title: Catechist's Guide — Justice
html:
Catechist's Guide

For preparing and leading the class. For catechist use only.

Lesson summary

Estimated duration: 55-65 minutes (with activities, 90 minutes)

Central theme: Justice is the cardinal virtue that gives each their due. It encompasses commutative justice (between persons), legal justice (from the citizen to the community), and distributive justice (from the community to the citizen). It is complemented by the Church's social doctrine: dignity of the person, common good, subsidiarity, and solidarity.

Objectives:

  • That the student understands justice is a demanding virtue, not a political opinion.
  • That they distinguish the three types of justice and the four principles of the SDC.
  • That they recognize the requirement of restitution as part of authentic conversion, following Zacchaeus's example.
  • That they discover the preferential option for the poor and the works of mercy as justice in action.

Key verse: Isaiah 32:17 («The effect of righteousness will be peace»)

Catechism references: CCC 1877-1948; complementary 2401-2463 (seventh commandment)

Suggested class structure

Section Duration What the catechist does
1. Welcome and ice-breaker 5 min Greet, create atmosphere. Question: «Have you ever felt you were treated unjustly? What happened?» Listen to their stories.
2. Zacchaeus's story 8 min Read Lk 19:1-10 aloud. Tell with details: who Zacchaeus was, why publicans were hated, the significance of climbing a tree, the fourfold restitution. Ask: «What do you think Zacchaeus felt when Jesus called him by name?»
3. Main content 25 min Present: the three types of justice (with everyday examples, not abstract), the SDC (four principles), the seventh commandment, economic sins, restitution, the option for the poor. Use the board to diagram the three types and four principles.
4. Witnesses of justice 8 min Tell Romero's conversion (brief but impactful: from timid bishop to prophet because he saw the corpses). Dorothy Day: the atheist journalist who became Catholic and lived among the poor. The Cristiada with St. José Sánchez del Río. Our Lady of Guadalupe as an icon of justice that restores dignity.
5. Group activity 10 min Choose a workbook activity. For children: explain Zacchaeus with drawings (the tree, the house, the money). For youth/adults: Zacchaeus's balance sheet (the table of what was received, owed, committed). If the group is mature: brief debate on whether you can be a good Christian while evading taxes or paying starvation wages.
6. Sharing 7 min Voluntary sharing of reflections. Don't force. Catechist closes.
7. Final prayer and closing 4 min Pray together the class prayer. Remember Is 32:17: opus iustitiae pax.

Notes for the catechist

About Zacchaeus and restitution

The story of Zacchaeus (Lk 19:1-10) is probably the best entry point for this topic. Students can connect with it in several ways. First, because Zacchaeus was a collaborator with the Empire — something that resonates with experiences of oppression and colonial power structures. Second, because the fourfold restitution (Lk 19:8) shows that justice is not an «extra» but the condition of salvation: «Today salvation has come to this house.» Third, because Zacchaeus was rich and short: ill-gotten wealth diminishes; restorative justice enlarges. Emphasis: restitution is not asked for by Jesus; it is offered by Zacchaeus. The encounter with Christ awakens the sense of justice. This is God's pedagogy.

About the Church's social doctrine

Many Catholics do not know the SDC or reduce it to a vague «the Church cares about the poor.» The SDC is much more precise. The four principles (dignity, common good, subsidiarity, solidarity) are a tool of discernment, not political slogans. You can illustrate them with concrete examples:

  • Dignity: refugees and migrants are not problems; they are persons with rights. Do we treat them that way?
  • Common good: public safety isn't solved only with more police; it requires education, dignified work, and effective justice.
  • Subsidiarity: the government does not replace parents in educating their children. But it must guarantee quality public schools when families cannot afford private education.
  • Solidarity: how did society respond to hurricanes, earthquakes, or other disasters? And to the daily disasters of poverty and homelessness?

About St. Romero

St. Óscar Arnulfo Romero (1917-1980) was canonized by Pope Francis in 2018. For years he was controversial because certain sectors accused him of being «Marxist» while others used him as a political banner. The truth is simpler and deeper: Romero was a man of the Church, of intense prayer, a lover of the Social Doctrine, who was radicalized by the reality of his people's suffering. His murder while celebrating Mass unites him to the Eucharistic mystery. Do not present him as a guerrilla or as a «political» saint, but as a pastor who understood that the Eucharist without justice toward the poor is incomplete. The film Romero (1989) is highly recommended for group viewing.

About Our Lady of Guadalupe and justice

This is the point where the class should be «Mexicanized» for groups in Mexico, but it also works universally. The Guadalupan apparitions were an event of justice. Mary appears to Juan Diego — a recently baptized Indian, without social power, without formal education — and not to Bishop Zumárraga. She speaks to him in his language. She calls him «Juanito, the least of my sons.» She promises her protection. And the tilma, which has lasted nearly 500 years defying the laws of physics, is the visible sign that God takes the side of the last. St. Juan Diego, canonized in 2002 by John Paul II, is the first indigenous saint of the Americas. God's justice chooses those the world discards.

Age adaptation

Children (7-12): Use only Zacchaeus's story. You can bring a paper tree and figurines for the children to act out the scene. The tree metaphor (climbing up to see Jesus, coming down to serve) works very well. Questions: «Have you taken something that wasn't yours? Did you give it back?» No abstractions: justice is giving back what you took and sharing what is yours.

Youth (13-17): Connect justice to their reality: bullying (broken commutative justice), academic dishonesty (copying, plagiarizing), bribery and nepotism. Use St. José Sánchez del Río's testimony (14 years old when he died) to show that justice costs. The film For Greater Glory has powerful scenes they can watch in fragments. Question: «How much is your faith worth? How far does your justice go when doing the right thing costs you?»

New adults: Tax evasion and the normalization of bribery are topics that will hit them directly. Honest question: «Is there 'small' corruption that isn't a sin?» The Catechism's answer is clear: no. Also the topic of unjust wages: many new adults are business owners, professionals, or employers and this teaching challenges them.

Formed adults: Go deeper into the justice-mercy relationship (Dives in Misericordia). The Thomistic distinction between commutative and distributive justice. The evolution of social thought from Leo XIII to Francis. And Gaudium et Spes as a whole as the theological framework.

Complementary resources

  • Compendium of the Social Doctrine of the Church: Free at vatican.va. The most authoritative summary of the SDC (2004).
  • YouCat 321-334: Youth catechism on property and social justice. Direct language for adolescents.
  • Documentary on St. Romero: «Monseñor Romero» (available on YouTube). 25 minutes, excellent.
  • Ascension Presents: Fr. Mike Schmitz's videos on Catholic social teaching.
  • Page of the Cause of the Cristeros: Biographical information on the canonized Mexican martyrs.
  • Nican Mopohua: The original Nahuatl account of the Guadalupan apparitions. Accessible translations exist.
  • Hakuna Group Music: Songs like «Justicia» and «Revolución» connect with youth.

Possible difficult questions (and how to answer them)

«So, is the Church socialist? Or capitalist?»

Neither. The Church does not marry any economic system. John Paul II said it clearly in Centesimus Annus: the Church does not propose an economic model, but moral principles that any model must respect. It criticizes socialism because it denies private property and freedom, and it criticizes unregulated capitalism because it treats people as commodities. The Church defends the social market economy, where the market is an instrument regulated by justice and solidarity at the service of the common good. Chesterton and Belloc proposed «distributism,» a Catholic third way: the widest possible diffusion of private property (so that it does not fall into few hands).

«If I have stolen and I repent, the confessor absolves me. Is that it?»

No. Sacramental forgiveness does not cancel the obligation to make restitution. The 1992 Catechism is clear on this (CCC 2412, 2454). Absolution forgives the sin; restitution repairs the justice. They are two different things. If you stole, you must return what was stolen (or its equivalent value) as soon as you can. If you can't now, you must resolve to do so as soon as possible. Confession is valid even if restitution has not been completed, but there must be a serious intention to repair. Without that purpose, the confession is invalid.

«In my country, if you're not corrupt, you can't get ahead. What do I do?»

This is the hardest question and the most real one for many. The Cristero martyrs did not die for abstractions; they died because in their concrete lives they preferred to lose everything rather than betray their faith. And many of them were poor peasants, not heroes. The answer isn't easy because sometimes doing the right thing has real consequences: losing a job, a contract, an opportunity. But the Gospel does not ask us to succeed; it asks us to be faithful. «What does it profit a man to gain the whole world and forfeit his soul?» (Mk 8:36). Moreover, corruption is not inevitable. Many people work honestly, pay their taxes, and reject bribes. You are not alone.

«The option for the poor — isn't that hatred of the rich?»

No. God loves the rich and wants to save them too. But he knows that wealth tends to harden the heart. The preferential option for the poor means that the Christian must look first at those who have the least and ask how every decision affects them. It is not hatred of the rich: it is priority for the poor. Zacchaeus didn't stop being rich to be saved. He gave half to the poor and returned what he had stolen. He remained rich, but his wealth was no longer unjust.

«The Cristiada was a war. Isn't killing a sin?»

The Church teaches the classic distinction on just war and legitimate defense (CCC 2302-2317). The Cristeros did not go out to kill; they went out to defend themselves from a persecution that had closed churches, murdered priests, and banned public worship. It was legitimate defense against an unjust aggression by the State. The Church has canonized several Cristeros, which means they died in a state of grace and their cause was just. But, like every war, it had excesses that the Church does not approve. The Cristero saints are saints because of their martyrdom, not their combat.

Supplementary notes on doctrinal content

Justice as a cardinal virtue and the other virtues

Justice is the second cardinal virtue after prudence, because prudence discerns what is owed and justice executes it. Without prudence, justice can become rigidity. Without justice, the other virtues limp: temperance becomes indifference, and fortitude becomes violence. The interdependence of the four cardinal virtues is a patristic teaching taken up by St. Thomas (STh II-II q.58).

The tension between universal destination and private property

This tension is deliberate. If you emphasize only the universal destination, you can fall into collectivism that denies personal freedom. If you emphasize only private property as an absolute right, you can justify obscene accumulation while others starve. The Catholic synthesis is: private property is a natural right derived from the person, but it is subordinated to the universal destination of goods. Vatican II (GS 69) expresses it with precision.

Work in John Paul II's theology

Laborem Exercens (1981) is probably the most important encyclical on work. John Paul II develops a «spirituality of work» rooted in Genesis. Human work participates in God's creative and redemptive work. But work is for man, not man for work. That is why a just wage is not a concession of the employer but a strict duty of justice. And unemployment is a social wound, not a statistic.

To prepare: Read CCC 1877-1948 carefully. Have your Bible open to Lk 19 and Mt 25. If you can, print a chart of the three types of justice and the four principles of the SDC to hand out.

Catechist's prayer

Lord, you who are the Just One par excellence,
who show no partiality,
who defend the poor and the helpless:
give me an upright heart to speak of justice without ideology,
and give me the wisdom to guide without manipulating.
Let me not be an obstacle so that my students,
like Zacchaeus, may come down from the tree and open their hands.
Amen.

Print Catechist's Guide
